Welcome back to Dry Powder. I\xe2\x80\x99m William D. Cohan. When I called up Larry Summers, the former Treasury secretary and president of Harvard University, to discuss the game of tariff chicken that Trump is playing with China, Canada, and Mexico, he called the stunt, rather fittingly, \xe2\x80\x9csurreal performance art.\xe2\x80\x9d Indeed, among the many Wall Street insiders I polled about this half-baked scheme, this was the prevailing sentiment.

Feb 05, 2025

William D. Cohan

Welcome back to Dry Powder. I\xe2\x80\x99m William D. Cohan .

When I called up Larry Summers , the former Treasury secretary and president of Harvard University, to discuss the game of tariff chicken that Trump is playing with China, Canada, and Mexico, he called the stunt, rather fittingly, \xe2\x80\x9csurreal performance art.\xe2\x80\x9d Indeed, among the many Wall Street insiders I polled about this half-baked scheme, this was the prevailing sentiment. Perhaps not surprisingly, though, many top bankers have a far more nuanced view of the current tariff sitch\xe2\x80\x94a topic that I\xe2\x80\x99ll be exploring tonight.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

  • Got an idea for you, Brian! : Peter Supino , the respected media analyst at Wolfe Research (and the nephew of my former boss at Lazard), fired a ballsy warning shot at Comcast C.E.O. Brian Roberts this week. In an open letter, Supino suggested that Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s controlling shareholder break up his company into three parts\xe2\x80\x94going much further than Brian\xe2\x80\x99s decision to create a \xe2\x80\x9cwell-capitalized\xe2\x80\x9d SpinCo composed of NBCU\xe2\x80\x99s cable networks, including both CNBC and MSNBC but minus Bravo. The Supino thesis, a considerable expansion on an idea he first floated five years ago, calls for carving out Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s cable distribution business and combining NBCU and Sky News, in addition to creating SpinCo.

    Notably, Supino\xe2\x80\x99s open letter followed Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s disappointing fourth-quarter earnings report, which revealed larger-than-anticipated losses in broadband subscribers, plus flat subscriber growth in its streaming business, causing the stock to tumble 11 percent in one day. (My partner Matt Belloni offered an acute analysis, as ever, in his recent piece Kabletown at a Krossroads .)

    Supino\xe2\x80\x99s note was unprecedented for its audacity, given the fact that the guy is challenging a seasoned management team from his perch as an analyst rather than as an activist investor with his own capital at stake. And, for what it\xe2\x80\x99s worth, it was filled with compelling ideas. For instance, Supino pointed out that Comcast peers Disney, WBD, and Charter all trade at higher EBITDA multiples (Disney trades at 13x estimated 2025 EBITDA, WBD trades at 6.7x, and Charter at 6.5x. Comcast, meanwhile, trades at 5.7x), noting that if Comcast were weighted proportionately and traded at an average of its multiples, its stock would rise 57 percent . \xe2\x80\x9cWe believe that the market discounts Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s value severely because of concerns about returns on capital and instances of misalignment of leadership and shareholders,\xe2\x80\x9d he wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cWe truly believe that as long as Comcast persists as a conglomerate, such concerns are likely to hamper the stock.\xe2\x80\x9d (Usual disclosure: This is not investment advice.)

    Supino doesn\xe2\x80\x99t see much growth potential in Xfinity, or from the broadcast networks, the cable networks, Peacock, and Sky, unless equity incentives are reshuffled to better align leadership and shareholders. He was also highly critical of Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s M&A strategy, estimating that Comcast spent $93 billion to acquire NBCU and Sky and to stand up Peacock, and that together they generate $7.5 billion of EBITDA, which the market values at 5.7x EBITDA, or $43 billion\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9ca $50 billion mark-to-market loss,\xe2\x80\x9d he wrote. He was also critical of Brian\xe2\x80\x99s $340 million in cumulative compensation over the last 10 years and his failure to invest in the Comcast stock during that time. His 40.8 million Comcast shares represent 1 percent of the shares outstanding. Meanwhile, he controls 31 percent of the vote.

    Supino wrapped up his jeremiad with some familiar notions. \xe2\x80\x9cWe believe Comcast\xe2\x80\x99s financial strength has provided false comfort to NBCU, and that the business independently funds its own growth investments,\xe2\x80\x9d he wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cWhile Universal has made great films and theme parks under Comcast and while Peacock is a beautiful service, NBC/Universal was late to streaming and lacks the scale to win against YouTube, Netflix, Amazon, and Disney. NBCU\xe2\x80\x99s EBITDA of more than $7B is more than enough to fund growth and negotiate potential strategic mergers.\xe2\x80\x9d

    Anyway, dividing Comcast into three parts is an interesting idea, to be honest, and one that Brian should consider seriously, even if he likely won\xe2\x80\x99t. Supino texted me yesterday that his report \xe2\x80\x9celicited more reads than any company-specific report I\xe2\x80\x99ve ever published. It\xe2\x80\x99s the only time in my career I\xe2\x80\x99ve ever written an opinion that elicited 100 percent positive feedback.\xe2\x80\x9d A spokesman for Comcast declined to comment.
  • The Wall Street revolving door : Two more senior Wall Street executives are leaving cushy posts to join the Trump administration. Last week, Trump announced that billionaire Stephen Feinberg , the C.E.O. of private equity firm Cerberus Capital Management, would be joining his administration as deputy secretary of defense, and that Morgan Stanley\xe2\x80\x99s Michael Grimes was headed to the Commerce Department in a yet-to-be-specified \xe2\x80\x9csenior role.\xe2\x80\x9d

    Grimes, the co-head of Morgan Stanley\xe2\x80\x99s technology banking group, is Elon Musk \xe2\x80\x99s favorite banker, or so they say. He famously steered the I.P.O.s of Facebook and Uber, and advised Musk on the $44 billion acquisition of Twitter. (That should get Grimes a demerit, not a job in Washington, but whatever.) Grimes will be working for Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick , the former C.E.O. of Cantor Fitzgerald, while Feinberg will be subordinate, unbelievably, to Pete Hegseth at D.O.D. (Feinberg had a stint during Trump\xe2\x80\x99s first administration as head of the President\xe2\x80\x99s Intelligence Advisory Board, whatever that is.)

    I rang up a former private equity bigwig who worked in the Biden administration to see if he had insight into why Feinberg and Grimes would leave their lofty perches to report to the likes of Hegseth and Lutnick. \xe2\x80\x9cThey get a lot of power, and it\xe2\x80\x99s cool,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. He also noted that both of these guys will probably avail themselves of the major benefits afforded people from the private sector who go to Washington in senior roles. Specifically, the ability to convert their net worth into Treasury securities on a tax-deferred basis, with the tax being paid only when the Treasuries are sold, if they are sold. It\xe2\x80\x99s a huge present-value savings, especially for a billionaire like Feinberg, who has enormous unrealized capital gains from creating Cerberus, and presumably, too, for Grimes, who has done extremely well at Morgan Stanley.

And now, the main event\xe2\x80\xa6

A Wall Street Soothsayer Translates the Tariffs

Other than the blanket pardons for the January 6 offenders, and Trump\xe2\x80\x99s D.E.I. press conference after the horrific helicopter-jet collision, the donor class on Wall Street is generally happy with Trump II.

William D. Cohan

Earlier this week, I was chatting with Larry Summers , the former Treasury secretary and president of Harvard University, from his home in Arizona. During our chat, I asked him to describe the game of tariff chicken that Donald Trump has been playing with China, Canada, and Mexico. \xe2\x80\x9cSurreal performance art would be the term that I would use,\xe2\x80\x9d Larry told me, without missing a beat, expanding on the opinion piece he co-authored last week in The Wall Street Journal with former senator Phil Gramm , urging Trump not to follow through with his tariff threats. (On Monday, Trump agreed to hold off for 30 days on imposing 25 percent tariffs on Mexico and Canada after extracting symbolic concessions from both countries.)


My conversations with bankers and other former government officials elicited similar responses. One Wall Street executive, who almost went to work for Trump this time around, artfully told me that the president was simply using the Oval Office and the media as his bully pulpit, itself a form of performance art. \xe2\x80\x9cBullies love to see people cower in fear,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cWhy be a bully if you can\xe2\x80\x99t do that? So how much of this is just, \xe2\x80\x98 Justin Trudeau , I\xe2\x80\x99m gonna fuck with him,\xe2\x80\x99 or \xe2\x80\x98Mexico didn\xe2\x80\x99t really do shit to stop immigration, time for them to shit their pants a little bit. Let\xe2\x80\x99s get a reset\xe2\x80\x99?\xe2\x80\x9d

A MESSAGE FROM GOLDMAN SACHS

Find a competitive edge with insights from Goldman Sachs' top economists and strategists.

The Outlooks provide a thorough examination of market trends, regional developments, and sector-specific forecasts.

Read the Outlooks.

A former Wall Streeter who did a stint in Washington described the first two weeks of Trump II as \xe2\x80\x9ca giant blender\xe2\x80\x9d of \xe2\x80\x9cfog and chaos\xe2\x80\x9d stirred up to distract people from three of Trump\xe2\x80\x99s near-term goals: getting his \xe2\x80\x9cidiot cabinet picks\xe2\x80\x9d through the Senate; positioning Russ Vought , Trump\xe2\x80\x99s pick to head the O.M.B., to \xe2\x80\x9ctake the shit out of the budget\xe2\x80\x9d; and getting congressional approval for the extension of the about-to-expire Trump I tax cuts. And, he said, Wall Street is on board. \xe2\x80\x9c[The tax cut extension] is what they really want,\xe2\x80\x9d he explained. \xe2\x80\x9cUntil he can\xe2\x80\x99t do that, they\xe2\x80\x99re perfectly happy to back him and focus on the things that warm their hearts, like shutting USAID. But if this goes on too long and/or the stock market goes down, they\xe2\x80\x99re not going to be so pleased.\xe2\x80\x9d

Another veteran Wall Street banker, who worked for Trump back in the day and knows him well, offered a more nuanced assessment of the tariff situation, as well as the view from the upper echelons of Wall Street, which has been conspicuously silent. There are two types of people who work for Trump in Washington, he told me\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9cthose who love tariffs, and those who are afraid to tell him the truth.\xe2\x80\x9d He said he has no idea what Trump \xe2\x80\x9cis trying to solve for,\xe2\x80\x9d but his hypothesis is that Trump was trying to follow through on a campaign promise and assuage his supporters who have lost manufacturing jobs, and who may believe that tariffs will reverse macroeconomic trends and reopen factories.

In this regard, the veteran banker viewed the president\xe2\x80\x99s motivations as genuine rather than theatrical: \xe2\x80\x9cHe thinks those people who lost those jobs, and those steel mills, should be back, because it\xe2\x80\x99s unfair that the Chinese are producing steel and polluting the air and not paying people living wages, and selling steel cheaper in the United States than we can produce it here.\xe2\x80\x9d Did he think it would work, I asked? \xe2\x80\x9cProbably not,\xe2\x80\x9d he averred. But it may not matter: Most voters are likely to remember the theater of the tariff threats, but fewer will notice when they are rolled back or postponed.


Trump, the banker continued, was only considering \xe2\x80\x9cthe part of the equation he wants to look at\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94obsessing over lost manufacturing jobs while seemingly ignoring that technology and service sector jobs have grown G.D.P. and lowered the unemployment rate during the past quarter-century. Indeed, many on Wall Street privately recognize that Trump has hyperbolically oversimplified a nuanced issue. To wit: Americans who rely on Walmart for low prices would never knowingly support tariffs that affect our supply chain relationships with Mexico and China, likely passing on the higher costs to them. \xe2\x80\x9cSo at the same time he\xe2\x80\x99s trying to help them, he\xe2\x80\x99s hurting them,\xe2\x80\x9d the banker said. \xe2\x80\x9cThe price of everything they\xe2\x80\x99re going to buy is going to go up dramatically\xe2\x80\x94tomorrow. And that steel mill, if it comes back, will take five to 10 years to come back.\xe2\x80\x9d

Sure, consumers could buy domestic\xe2\x80\xa6 in theory. But there are many commodities for which there is no \xe2\x80\x9cBuy American\xe2\x80\x9d option. \xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t make athletic shoes in the United States,\xe2\x80\x9d my smart Wall Street source explained. \xe2\x80\x9cYou can buy a Nike, or you can buy an On, or you can buy a New Balance, but they\xe2\x80\x99re all made offshore.\xe2\x80\x9d The same goes for cellphones, which are mostly made in China, and a million other consumer goods. \xe2\x80\x9cThat cellphone that retails for $1,500 in the United States, it\xe2\x80\x99s made for $500 to $600 in China,\xe2\x80\x9d he continued, noting the complexity of global supply chains and the depth of Asia\xe2\x80\x99s manufacturing base. \xe2\x80\x9cIf we needed to make that cellphone in the United States, it would cost over $2,000, and it would retail for $4,000.\xe2\x80\x9d


Trump not only dismisses or misunderstands these obstacles, he also underestimates the resulting inflationary pressure, both from the tariffs themselves and any retaliatory actions by other countries\xe2\x80\x94which are inevitable. \xe2\x80\x9cHe does believe tariffs work, but when he sees the reaction, he somehow is always surprised,\xe2\x80\x9d the banker explained. \xe2\x80\x9cHe keeps telling people that there shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be a reaction, but there is a reaction. Then he backs off\xe2\x80\x9d and claims a concession. He told me that he suspects White House staff were working overtime with the Mexicans and the Canadians to extract anything that could be spun by Trump as a quick victory. (The White House did not respond to my request for comment.)

A Wall Street Bet

Amid our conversation, I wondered, did the financially sophisticated people around Trump overlook these issues, or were they playing a longer game? And here, in particular, this veteran banker had a fascinating view.

A MESSAGE FROM GOLDMAN SACHS

Goldman Sachs economists and strategists share insights on the key factors driving the global economy.

What to expect: Market-by-market, region-by-region, sector-by-sector analysis for the year ahead.

Read the Outlooks.

Wall Street doesn\xe2\x80\x99t like or want tariffs, per se \xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9cThere are only losers with tariffs,\xe2\x80\x9d the banker said\xe2\x80\x94but many accept them as part of the price of doing business with Trump. Indeed, many machers preferred Trump II over Biden or Kamala Harris . \xe2\x80\x9cTo me, it was a prisoner\xe2\x80\x99s dilemma,\xe2\x80\x9d this person continued before articulating the internal monologue in the highest ranks of the banking industry. \xe2\x80\x9cWe had an anti-business Biden opportunity, or a pro-business Trump, who may put tariffs on, or may not . But if he puts them on and he realizes they\xe2\x80\x99re bad, he may take them off within 24 hours. So they said, \xe2\x80\x98We\xe2\x80\x99d rather deal with Donald Trump because he\xe2\x80\x99ll take our phone calls and we can tell him how bad these are, whereas Joe Biden won\xe2\x80\x99t take our phone calls.\xe2\x80\x99 Corporate America felt like we could sway Trump. We could get to him, and convince him that these tariffs are wrong.\xe2\x80\x9d

In fact, other than the blanket pardons for the January 6 offenders, and Trump\xe2\x80\x99s press conference where he blamed the fatal helicopter-jet collision near the Potomac on D.E.I., the donor class on Wall Street is generally happy with Trump II, according to this banker, a reality that much of the media still refuses to comprehend. \xe2\x80\x9cThere are very good reasons to criticize D.E.I., but in this context, it\xe2\x80\x99s a little insane,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, referring to Trump\xe2\x80\x99s post-crash commentary. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd insulting and in bad taste.\xe2\x80\x9d On the other hand, he continued, \xe2\x80\x9ceveryone is thrilled\xe2\x80\x9d that the \xe2\x80\x9capple cart\xe2\x80\x9d at USAID is getting turned over and that people are seeing who is getting paid what from the Treasury\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9csort of all that DOGE stuff,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, which Wall Street types are giving \xe2\x80\x9ca standing ovation.\xe2\x80\x9d


But given the largely foreseeable chaos of the past few weeks, was there anything else that motivated the Wall Street donor class to lean so heavily into Trump last November, I wondered? \xe2\x80\x9cThey don\xe2\x80\x99t think being rich is enough,\xe2\x80\x9d one of my sources told me. \xe2\x80\x9cThey think they need to be venerated for being rich, and Biden didn\xe2\x80\x99t venerate them for being rich. The idea that wealth equals merit tends to be appealing to the wealthy. They want wealth accumulation to be seen as making them like Einstein or Leonardo da Vinci . These guys are trying to be Averell Harriman , but they\xe2\x80\x99re going to end up being Bebe Rebozo .\xe2\x80\x9d

An insider-friendly tip sheet from Matthew Belloni, who spent 14 years in the trenches at The Hollywood Reporter and five before that as an entertainment lawyer. Subscribers also receive What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing+, a companion email focused on entertainment law, the streaming industry, and more.

Puck senior political correspondent Tara Palmeri is here to help you get through what may be the most chaotic and consequential presidential election cycle of our lifetime. With 15 years covering politics, Tara speaks with the smartest political brains to discuss what is happening behind the scenes in Washington D.C., and on the campaign trail.

Zaslav\xe2\x80\x99s Superman Problem

ERIQ GARDNER

Nike\xe2\x80\x99s New Playbook

SARAH SHAPIRO

Tavitian\xe2\x80\x99s Gilded Age

JULIE BRENER DAVICH
